exemplar.title: To what extent has the representation of female characters in Disney movie scripts evolved through the 4 waves of feminism from 1959-2012: A comparison of Sleeping Beauty and Brave

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the representation of female characters in Disney movie scripts evolved through the 4 waves of feminism from 1959-2012: A comparison of Sleeping Beauty and Brave

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: Clear and sustained communication of the research question and investigation focus.
Appropriate comparative methodology consistently applied across sections.
Concise background framing and coherent purpose evident throughout the essay.
Wellâformulated research question with clear connection to analysis and conclusion.
weaknesses: Methodological framework (e.g., Proppâs functions) is not fully or systematically integrated.
Reliance on popular rather than peerâreviewed sources limits academic depth.
Comparative framework is not consistently reiterated in later sections.
Justification for selection of primary data extracts could be more explicit.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a precise and effective articulation of topic and research question throughout the essay, with a coherent comparative method and clear purpose. However, occasional methodological lapsesâuneven source selection, incomplete integration of narrative frameworks, and minor drift into general contextâpreclude the highest mark. Overall, performance aligns solidly with the top of band 3â4 and lower band 5â6 descriptors, meriting a 5.

strengths: Accurate, consistent use of key subjectâspecific terminology (e.g., archetype, patriarchy).
Coherent, wellâorganized knowledge of primary films and feminist theory.
Detailed contextual understanding of feminist waves and Disneyâs cultural influence.
Effective application of concepts such as the Bechdel Test and narrative archetype.
weaknesses: Overreliance on journalistic/popular sources reduces scholarly authority.
Minor imprecisions in terminology (e.g., âcooperationâ vs. âcorporationâ).
Occasional historical inaccuracies in describing feminist wave chronology.
Insufficient use of primary film theory literature for key definitions.

Occasional slips in precision and dependence on nonâscholarly sources prevent an âexcellentâ rating, placing the work firmly in the 3â4 band with characteristics edging toward band 5â6âwarranting a final score of 5.

feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets all IB requirements with a clear structure, consistent formatting, and effective navigation aids, placing it squarely in the top band (3â4). Minor lapses in citation consistency and paragraphing prevent absolute perfection but do not detract from overall readability or compliance, meriting the maximum score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
â¢ The student adopts a clear comparative methodology: close script/scene analysis of two primary films supported by secondary commentary on feminism and narrative theory (e.g., Bechdel Test, Proppâs functions).
â¢ Source range is serviceable but uneven; most secondary material comes from popular websites (Psychology Today, History.com) rather than peer-reviewed scholarship, limiting academic depth.
â¢ Method is applied consistently, yet some analytical tools (e.g., Propp) are introduced briefly and not systematically integrated; thus, methodology is mostly complete and relevant but lacks the breadth and critical rigour expected for the highest band.

â¢ Consistent use of headings, font, spacing and page numbering meets IB EE guidelines; the stated word count confirms compliance with length limits.
â¢ In-text citations and a works-cited list are present; though citation style varies slightly and some bibliographic details are incomplete, overall referencing is clear enough for verification.
â¢ Visual presentation is neat and readable; minor inconsistencies in citation formatting (e.g., italics, dates) prevent a flawless impression but remain within the highest band descriptors.

â¢ The investigation is grounded in relevant primary sources (the two films) and reinforced by secondary materials that directly address feminism, Disney studies and narrative theory, ensuring a strong linkage to the research question.
â¢ While many references are popular rather than scholarly, they remain pertinent; most evidence cited (dialogue excerpts, scene descriptions, definitions of feminist concepts) supports the central argument effectively.
â¢ Some key academic perspectives on feminist film theory are missing, and a few citations lack full publication data, tempering the level of excellence and placing this strand solidly in the âgoodâ (7â9) range.

â¢ The topicâhow Disneyâs female characters reflect changing feminist wavesâis stated explicitly in the title, the opening paragraph and revisited in the comparison and conclusion, giving the reader a clear and consistent sense of purpose throughout the essay.
â¢ Explanatory background on Disneyâs cultural influence and on the four waves of feminism is concise yet sufficient, allowing the reader to understand the investigationâs scope without ambiguity.
â¢ Minor lapses in precision (e.g., calling Disney a âcooperation,â conflating first-wave with the 1950s) do not seriously impede clarity, so overall communication is accurate, purposeful and sustained.

â¢ A coherent line of argument runs from introduction through comparison to conclusion, clearly answering the research question and synthesising findings across both films.
â¢ The student offers evaluative commentsâsuch as assessing the Bechdel Testâs implications and noting Disneyâs alignment with contemporaneous societal normsâthat show developing critical judgement.
â¢ Evaluation of limitations and consideration of different feminist or film-theory perspectives are limited, and claims occasionally rely on assertion rather than evidence, keeping the discussion at the lower end of the âgoodâ band.

â¢ The essay moves beyond description to purposeful analysisâe.g., unpacking figurative language in Sleeping Beauty and exploring how costume symbolism in Brave reflects Meridaâs resistance.
â¢ Comparative insights (e.g., differing emphases on female appearance, romance vs. maternal bonds) reveal thoughtful engagement with the texts.
â¢ Nonetheless, alternative readings and counter-arguments are only briefly mentioned, and some assertions (e.g., linking every device to feminist âwavesâ) could be more rigorously substantiated; thus analysis is âgoodâ rather than consistently âexcellentâ.

â¢ All required structural elementsâtitle page, abstract substitute, table of contents, clearly headed sections, conclusion and works-citedâare present and logically ordered, facilitating reader navigation.
â¢ Paragraphing is generally coherent, and the comparative structure (individual film analyses followed by an explicit comparison) is entirely appropriate for the research question.
â¢ Minor issues (e.g., occasional lengthy paragraphs and some abrupt transitions) are evident but do not impair the overall suitability of the structure, justifying the top band for presentation.

â¢ Detailed plot and dialogue references from both films demonstrate a strong, coherent grasp of the primary material and of the socio-historical context of each feminist wave.
â¢ Secondary sources are generally accurate and used to frame the argument; however, reliance on journalistic or popular sites slightly weakens scholarly authority, preventing a fully âexcellentâ rating.
â¢ Overall, the essay displays confident, well-organised knowledge of Disneyâs oeuvre, feminist theory, and narrative conventions, applied effectively to support the comparison.

â¢ Subject-specific termsâarchetype, Bechdel Test, Proppâs functions, patriarchy, wave of feminismâare used accurately and woven naturally into analysis of dialogue and scenes.
â¢ Concepts such as gender stereotype, narrative archetype and feminist wave theory are applied consistently to interpret textual evidence, showing confident command of disciplinary language.
â¢ Minor slips in phrasing (e.g., âcooperationâ for âcorporationâ) occur but do not detract significantly from the overall precision and consistency of terminology.

â¢ The research question is prominently displayed on the title page and echoed in the introduction and conclusion, demonstrating a clear through-line linking all sections of the essay.
â¢ Its focusâtwo specific films across clearly defined feminist wavesâgives the investigation a manageable and appropriate scope for a 4,000-word EE.
â¢ Occasional drift occurs when the essay references general Disney history or broad feminist context without tying it back to the core comparison, so the connection is strong but not flawlessly sustained, meriting the lower end of the top band.

annotations.comment: The introduction succinctly contextualizes Disneyâs cultural impact and the societal concerns around gender stereotypes, showing coherent topic knowledge.
The overview of Brave connects Meridaâs characterization to fourth-wave feminist ideals, demonstrating clear contextual and historical understanding.
The close reading of Meridaâs exclamations demonstrates careful attention to the characterâs narrative voice and emotional stakes.
Analyzing the punctuation and pronoun choice in âI want you back, mummy. I love you.â shows nuanced consideration of emotional impact.
The conclusion here summarizes findings well but omits discussion of study limitations and avenues for further research, which would deepen critical reflection.
The title precisely frames the investigationâs scope by naming Disney, feminist waves, and specific films, establishing a clear purpose and focus for the essay.
The table of contents is comprehensive and logically organized, enhancing navigation and reflecting an effective structural layout.
The term âcooperationâ is misused instead of âcorporation,â indicating imprecise use of subject-specific terminology.
The citation â(Britannica.)â omits author, date, and publication details, reducing the scholarly reliability of the source reference.
The research question is explicitly stated and appropriately narrowed to two films and four feminist waves, ensuring a focused and coherent investigation.
The inclusion of a word count confirms compliance with the 4,000-word limit, demonstrating meticulous adherence to IB formatting requirements.
Stating that feminism âonly made an impact in the 1960âsâ overlooks first-wave achievements and introduces a chronological inaccuracy into the historical overview.
Several sentences run on without clear breaks, which can impede reader comprehension; consider shorter, more focused constructions.
While the chosen figurative passages are compelling, the essay should explicitly justify their selection in relation to the research question to strengthen methodological rigor.
The analysis of metaphors like âgold of sunshineâ and similes such as âlips that shame the red red roseâ demonstrates strong engagement with textual evidence.
Linking the fairy godmothersâ domestic roles to 1950s housewife stereotypes provides insightful socio-historical critique and enriches the analysis.
The discussion would benefit from examining counter-instances of female agency in Sleeping Beauty to avoid a one-sided critique.
Proppâs narrative functions are introduced but not systematically applied to each character; deepen the frameworkâs integration for methodological completeness.
The application of the Bechdel Test effectively underscores Sleeping Beautyâs outdated gender dynamics and aligns with the historical analysis.
Relying solely on Merriam-Webster for the Bechdel Test definition omits its feminist theoretical origins; incorporate primary film theory literature.
The comparative methodology is not reiterated in the Brave section; restating the analytical framework would reinforce coherence.
Using Meridaâs dialogue âcurse this dressâ to illustrate her rebellion against princess archetypes effectively grounds the argument in primary evidence.
This section would be strengthened by incorporating peer-reviewed scholarship on female agency in animation, rather than relying mainly on popular sources.
The analysis here mostly describes plot events rather than evaluating how they engage with broader feminist film theory; deepen the critical interrogation.
The conclusion succinctly synthesizes the research question and key findings, providing clear closure and reaffirming the investigationâs focus.
The conclusion would benefit from acknowledging reliance on popular sources and suggesting more scholarly literature for future investigations.
Works Cited entries show inconsistent formatting, with uneven italics and missing dates; adopt a consistent citation style throughout.
The comparative section methodically juxtaposes thematic focus and character agency across both films, reflecting a coherent comparative framework.
Transitions between comparative points are sparse; integrating clearer signposting would improve the logical flow of the argument.
